speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 14, 1995. We're taking testimony from William Levy. The interviewer will be Ellen Baskin. We're in Valley Village, California, United States of America, and, uh, it will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 14, 1995. We're going to be taking the testimony of William Levy. We're in Valley Village, California, in the United States of America. My name is Ellen Baskin, and the language will be English. I'd like to begin by asking you to tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: William Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wowa Lejwi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your name at birth for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-O-W-A L-E-J-W-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Lejwi now?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-V-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrog, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in Poland was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Ukraine, on the border from Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was-- now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, what-- and what was it then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And my mother's named Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one older brother. His name was Zusi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-U-S-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years older than you was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had-- my mom and dad had a small business, like mom and pop store. It was from-- call it today-- milk products-- milk, butter, eggs, sour cream, sweet cream, eggs, those kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had a dairy business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like-- yeah. In a small way, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they get the dairy products?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad used to have a couple of places in the villages. And he used to go a couple of times a week-- two times, three times a week. And he  used to buy there. He was known. He used to buy and then bring it to the city. And of course, in those days we had no ice bucket. I mean, refrigeration. So what we rented was a very deep, deep cellar they kept there, and it was very, very cold. It kept it fresh, till they needed for us to sell it in the store. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go with your father to get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I went many times. My dad-- especially in the summer time when school was out, I would go with him on this places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from place to place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horse and buggy, It was those days, you know. Had no truck, no car. Matter of fact, in the whole city was one car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose car was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: One man-- one rich man, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a city or town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh. It was a provincial city-- town I guess. I really wouldn't know the amount of people, really. I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it predominantly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a big amount of population-- Jewish population-- but mostly Ukrainian. And of course, I would say about 35% of Pollacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have extended family-- aunts and uncles, grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had two sisters and parents at the time when I was born, or a couple years. And, uh, my grandfather died when I was three or four. I don't remember. And my grandmother was alive till I-- till I left from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your grandparent's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miilfiirer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their first names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was Leb-- L-E-B-- Leb. I think that's what it was. And my grandmother's name was Libi-- L-I-B-I or something like that. B-E-- I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you spell their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like my grandmother spelled it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- which is  how?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-I-L-F-I-I-R-E-R..

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you travel with your family or go away at all for holidays? Or did--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: To the mountains or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: What mountain? No. This wasn't-- how do you say? In those days, no way that I-- maybe, I don't know. We never went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun when you were  a small boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we do for fun? Well, the kids [LAUGHS] in the neighborhood would play all kinds of sports. We made our own sports, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soccer on the yard, some maybe the school yard or something like that. We played-- I don't even know  how you call it. It's called [NON-ENGLISH]. It's almost-- uh, some kind of like a baseball or something. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to elementary school. And I finished-- those days were seven grades. I finished seven grades. Until about the time the summer when the war started, when the Russian took over part of Poland and the Germans took over part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took over the part that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian side, because I was in eastern-- like we say, it's eastern Europe, eastern Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. 1939 in the summer. I'm not sure. I think it was in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did things change in your life then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two bed-- two rooms,   which is a two bedroom apartment-- called two bedroom. When the Russians came, they took away one bedroom for an officer. So we had to live, all four of us, in one room. In the kitchen, you know, it was probably all right, but we didn't have running water in the house and no electricity. [? It was ?] [? a nicer ?] part those days. We didn't have it. But they took-- still took away a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they do this just to--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took away the business, everything. It was [INAUDIBLE], you know,

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they do that just to the Jews? No, to everybody. The Russians come in. They took away everything. Took all the stores away-- everything. When you were growing up, before the Russians came, was there any mistreatment of the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. It was-- inside the city was living a lot of Jews. And we didn't experience any trouble-- anti-Semite trouble. We  never experienced this. I don't think so. I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father do business with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. All this business was with non-Jews, until the Russians came, of course. Otherwise, there was no Jews. There was Ukrainian people, you know. Czechs and Ukrainian. Never had trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did your father do when the Russians took his business? Did he work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked. He worked like a night watchman. It was hard. It was a hard life. A very hard life. Everything changed completely. I still went to school a little bit, in the to night school. And, uh, after that, there was no way out, so I went to school in a different city. Was-- they made announcement in the paper, I think it  was, if I remember, that [INAUDIBLE] supposedly go and learn to be a director-- railroad station director. And you went through just like the army. They give you a physical-- complete physical and everything. And they send you to a different city. And maybe I don't know about-- maybe 200 kilometers away, maybe more-- A city called Korets. And we was there, but there wasn't much schooling. It was a lot of work. We had to build a railroad. Build a railroad. And we divide into groups, one for the railroad tracks, one for the-- I don't know what you call it-- the lines on top of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The rails.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rails put up. And you know people in the soil, prepared the soil for the railroad. And you have a few hours lessons supposedly teaching you, but you just work like a dog. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this supervised by Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: By Russians, of course. Russian-- from the Russia-- I'm sure-- I think it was the Communist party or something like that. It was-- They called them-- I don't know what you call them. Teachers, whatever you want to call them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when is it that you left to go to Korets?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940. 1940 in the summer, I think was my first summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you have an awareness of Nazis, as opposed to the other soldiers whom you were encountering?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't encounter the Nazis. I was there in the city, and we had an announcement from our teacher, the supervisor, that said, the war started. We know it-- the day after, we know it that the war started because somebody was sitting by a canal like that. And me and my two friends were sitting and talking. And we saw a plane coming down. It starts shooting with its, uh, machine gun. And then we just lay down in the-- in this canal. And nothing happened to us. Just the one-- the-- the bullet went through the coat, which was-- coat-- it went through a coat, but didn't touch us. So we know that the was is there. And the next day, the supervisor, or the teacher, came and tell us, the Germans try to start a war with us, and they're close by. We've got to move. At midnight we've got to move away from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when you were in Korets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. 1940. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we walked, uh, just about a night and a day and maybe two days and a night. We walked all the way to Poltava. And on the way, of course, was a lot of peopling walking. And everybody took whatever they put with them. But I guess they couldn't carry it for so long. And you saw a lot of suitcases and all kind of stuff lying in the road. And nobody take-- like, years later maybe the natives took [INAUDIBLE]. But just a lot of stuff-- clothes, everything. And we went and the-- the planes came-- the German planes-- and started shooting. And the people. was running and hiding in the high grow. There was corn and [INAUDIBLE] stalk were grown very high because it was middle of the summer, in June, I think it was. And whoever got the bullet didn't get up. Whoever not, got up and continued to go until he came to the Poltava station-- the city there, to the rail station. And it was a mess. I never saw such a thing in my life-- in My short life what I had. | never saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it  like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands and thousands of people on the rail station. And everybody scream, father, mother, sister, brother, screaming to go to where their families are. And of course, then the plane came and started shooting. And the soldiers coming back already from the front, and sitting on the open box cars where they arrived. And they had an all-bandaged heads everywhere and the hand, and the legs. And they started shooting with the rifles away to the place. Can you imagine? And we stayed until it got dark. Then we had a train, a box car train, and we continued go to deep Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom were you with at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a couple of friends. But a lot of those kids, of course, we were together in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you tried to contact your family or get back to your family at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't because the Germans were first in our city where I was born. Before they went to this-- to the city where I was in school, so I couldn't go home. I just had to continue. I find out later, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point you didn't know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not at all. I knew nothing about  my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh, uh, couple of days-- probably two or three days and two nights, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a boxcar train going very slow. We went all the way to very deep Russia to Chkalov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can try. C-H-K-A-L-O-W. We came down-- this was, uh, like a, um, central railroad which went to Siberia. It went to Asia. From this point, the central point, you could go almost everywhere in Russia. And we came there, and we stayed there just a couple of days-- a day or a couple of days. When we went-- the train where we were, in this box car, we went to Siberia to Novo-- by Novosibirsk to Sara station.

speaker: interviewer
text: S-- could you spell Sara?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-A-R-A. We were there-- it's still the same kids we were in school. OK. And on the train-- on this boxcar train-- was a textile factory from Kharkov. It was evacuated there. And we came there as they started to say, to unload the boxcar, the machinery, the material-- it was a lot of the material there from the factory and everything to unload there. And we unloaded. It was a lot of [INAUDIBLE]. And he says, who wants to be a night watchman to watch the stores that nobody steals them? And we watched it. We helped ourselves a little bit then. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: To what?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you help yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Materials, you know, and we sell it to the natives. And for this one you could buy some food, but  there was no food there, nothing. You couldn't buy-- there was special restaurants for the executives, for the high military, for the others. So you get to the [INAUDIBLE] you give to waiters something, you know, so he gives you some food and everything else, you know. So you could survive. And we met a woman, an older woman. All three of us were together. She says, don't stay in this part of the country because come winter, it's very, very cold. You're going to freeze to death. Go to Tashkent. I didn't know where Tashkent was. So we talked, the three of us together and says, we've got to leave. Matter of fact, I want to mention, where we lived and sleepped on a side lines, were boxcars-- opened boxcars. You know, those boxcars. We slept in the boxcars. This was, uh, where we slept.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came one night, the three of us, it was about maybe midnight, one o'clock at night. We went to the station. It wasn't far, right? We went to the station, and then we talked to the girl that was on the rail station there-- three tickets. She says, I'm sorry. I cannot give you. You outta to have from the government-- what do you call it? Papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: A paper, you know, that you are allowed to go, you know. We didn't have it. So I asked her, where is the director of the station? [INAUDIBLE] he says, go around to  the other room. So I went. There was sitting an old Russian Kossack with the-- and he asked me, what do you want, punk? You know, like in Russian. I said, I need three tickets, and I had then under my jacket, I had a big bottle of vodka. I gave him the vodka, and he says-- and he screamed to the girl in the front, and they gave us three tickets. And the train came maybe in 15, 20 minutes. We took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your friend's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, lord. I don't-- one was-- last name was [INAUDIBLE], and one Gelman. [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] Gelman. I saw him after the war. He was married to a Russian Jewish girl. I think was Jewish. I'm not sure. But I saw him-- just him-- for a short while. I think it was in [? Waldenburg. ?] I'm not sure. But the other guy I never saw again. We were apart and--. We stayed-- we went with the train. We came all the way to Chkalov, back. And from Chkalov we took our train. There was a train. We went to middle Asia, to Tashkent. We just somehow made it on this train, and we went to Asia to-- to-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Tashkent was in Asia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tashkent, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that trip take?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days. Of course, Obviously was thousands of miles. So we came to Tashkent. We wanted to go down. We saw the railroad station on the steps was a tragedy. There was people lying dead, people on the steps. There was a typhus there. And we said, no, we aren't going down here. We will continue. I don't know where, but we're going to continue. And we continued for another couple of hours. I don't know  where or how long. We came to a different state or a different city, and we went down.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I'm not sure. It was [? Leninsk. ?] We went done there. We went to the police station, [NON-ENGLISH]. And all three of us, we talked to the guy. He was very nice, [INAUDIBLE], and he gave us some food. We ate. We talked. Then he sent us to work to the collective farm. We collective farm, we worked a few days. I don't know, maybe a couple of weeks. I don't-- I can't recall how long-- a few weeks. And I had the misfortune, a scorpion bit me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a scorpion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. On the farm, we w-- we worked by the grape-- grapes trees. Grapes was lying like that on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grapes, yeah. But what I did-- I know it-- I took a razor blade, and I cut it, and I took out-- sucked out all the stuff from the pain. And I run to the clinic-- the place where I knew there were doctors. So I came there, and he says, what did you do? I said to him. He says, there's nothing else to do. You can go back home. I did the right thing. I just sucked out the poison, and that's it. Put a bandage of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were you doing with the grapes?

speaker: interviewee
text: You clean around the trees around the soil and all that. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the  living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm asking. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was there guys. And there was one room with bunks, you know, bed bunks like that without any covers, without blankets, just lay in bed bunks. It was mighty cold so we-- we found some old furniture and made fire when you heat it. But after a very short time-- excuse me-- I don't remember how long, a few weeks maybe or so-- we were called back to the-- to the police there. And this-- this captain there-- whatever he was in the police-- he told us, listen guys, in a couple of days, tomorrow, after tomorrow, you're going to get a paper. You have to go to the war battalion. We couldn't go to the army. It was too young-- to the war battalion. You have a choice, [INAUDIBLE] or just come register. So we went back there. And we talked it over. We were on our way. We're not going to war battalion. So we left. We left. We went to the train and went to a different city. I don't remember what the name was there. We went there, and somehow we separated. I can't remember what happened. They went a different direction. I went a different direction. I went to Leninabad. Lenina--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lenin, L-I-N-I-B-A-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, but missed a city in Uzbek or Tajik, the language there-- so Leninabad, Stalingrad, '-bad,' you know  and all that. Anyway, so they left, and I went there. And, uh, I was in the market then and saw some people from my city, where I was born. And they told me, come over there. Come there. So we came there. I-- me, I came there and-- with some people I know before the war. And we-- I stayed there. I slept on the floor, on the soil, you know. I slept there, maybe 10 guys in the room. And [INAUDIBLE] they arranged for me to get that-- those Russian tickets. Every day you received about 400 gram of bread. And the bread was [INAUDIBLE]. It was so heavy you could put in one hand, the bread, I mean hold it, because it was very wet. It was soggy and heavy. So that's what they ate, you know, and tried to see what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when was this? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. '41. It all happened fast. You  know, [INAUDIBLE]. And then I, uh-- I start to get acquainted with the mayor from the city-- there was a small city there. And I bought some tickets, bread tickets. And I went to the store and buy maybe a couple kilos of bread and sold it on the black market. And with this I could live a little bit to buy me some other food, whatever, you know. Not a big deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day two agents, they caught me. I put the tickets in under my hat. And [INAUDIBLE] they took me under the arms, and they took the-- they took off the hat to find the tickets. Where did you get the tickets from? I said, I found it. What can you say? You cannot say, I bought it from the mayor. So I told them I found it. And they didn't believe me anyways. They took me to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the police. And it was the captain there, or whatever it was, a sergeant, whatever it was. And he says, where you got the tickets from? I said, I   found them. Throw him in jail. Took off my shoelaces, you know, everything what you can-- and put me in jail. Took away all the-- what I had-- a little bit  of money. Whatever I had, he took away, and he threw me in jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a few guys in jail already, but for different acts what they did. I was there-- they caught me  about 10:00 in the morning. It was about a whole day almost. And they gave no food, no nothing, nothing. I was lucky because those three guys had a girlfriend there through the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guys from your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the guy who were in the-- in the-- in the prison.

words.text: the-- in the-- in the prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in jail with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jail, yeah. And then [INAUDIBLE] she brought them bread and salami and all that. And the guy asked me, hey, punk, you want to eat something? You hungry? I said, yeah. I was [INAUDIBLE] since the morning. So he broke off a piece of bread, a piece of  salami. He gave it to me, I ate it. It became night. And they slept-- there was a big table. They slept on top of the table. I slept under the table in the room. And the police came at night and kicked me, and said, where did you get tickets? You know, and they kicked me, all time, kicking me. I say, I found it. What can I say? Anyways, they leave me alone until the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 10:00, 11:00 o'clock in the morning-- was it? I don't remember-- The police came in, says, go into the Captain. He calls you to the office. I went in there, and he says, OK, now, I'll let you go. You go now to the market and find the guy who sold you the tickets. And when you find them, bring-- come back. Bring them back. When I came out of the prison, some guys from my people met me and says, listen, we know what happened. We gave them 2,000 rubles, and we fixed it up. So I've never been back to the market to sell [INAUDIBLE]. I was scared to death. But did the policeman know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did their treatment-- No, not really. It doesn't really make difference. Black market here, you know. So I went no more to school. I was looking for a way out. This was 1942 already. And I find out that in-- in Lenin-- in the city-- this was, like, on the railroad, a little small town, but I went to the city. And I find the out that the Polish-- like not embassy, but organization. And they have a school. I enter in that. So I went there and talked to them. And they took me in there. So it was like you slept there, and you ate, and you went to school and all that. So I was there for a little while. How old were you at this time? About?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17. And they told me already that you cannot be much longer because until 18 years of age. So the men there [INAUDIBLE] says, how about you go learn a trade? We're going to teach you a trade, and you go out on your own and working.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he put me into the-- I wanted to go-- two choices he says you have. You're going to learn cinematography-- 'Kino' means movie, movie camera, you know in those days. Movie camera-- or you can be a truck driver. Now, unfortunately, in those days, a truck driver was much more worth than cinematography. I wish I look cinematography at the time. But I did truck driving because truck driving, you made a lot of money. So I went to school there, and I finished the course there for a driver. And I was driving like a second hel-- helper on a truck with somebody else with a man I know from Poland too, a Jewish man. And I drove with him for a year. And then I drove myself there for awhile. Anyway, I-- between time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] find out that my mom and my brother are in Asia, but far away there. They're by Buhkara, somewhere there-- Samarkand? Buhkara? I think it was Buhkara. They are there in a collective farm. OK. We're going to change tapes now.
